[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/74/MAOA_Gene_with_ChrX_Ideogram.svg/250px-MAOA_Gene_with_ChrX_Ideogram.svg.png)](/wiki/File:MAOA_Gene_with_ChrX_Ideogram.svg)

MAOA gene is located on the short (p) arm of the [X chromosome](/wiki/X_chromosome "X chromosome") at position 11.3.

**Monoamine oxidase A**, also known as **MAO-A**, is an [enzyme](/wiki/Enzyme "Enzyme") ([E.C.](/wiki/Enzyme_classification "Enzyme classification") 1.4.3.4) that in [humans](/wiki/Human "Human") is encoded by the *MAOA* [gene](/wiki/Gene "Gene").[[5]](#cite_note-pmid1678250-5)[[6]](#cite_note-pmid2023912-6) This gene is one of two neighboring gene family members that encode [mitochondrial](/wiki/Mitochondria "Mitochondria") enzymes which [catalyze](/wiki/Catalyze "Catalyze") the [oxidative](/wiki/Oxidative "Oxidative") [deamination](/wiki/Deamination "Deamination") of [amines](/wiki/Amine "Amine"), such as [norepinephrine](/wiki/Norepinephrine "Norepinephrine"), [serotonin](/wiki/Serotonin "Serotonin") and [tyramine](/wiki/Tyramine "Tyramine"). A mutation of this gene results in [Brunner syndrome](/wiki/Brunner_syndrome "Brunner syndrome"). This gene has also been associated with a variety of other psychiatric disorders, including [antisocial](/wiki/Anti-social_behavior "Anti-social behavior") behavior. [Alternatively spliced](/wiki/Alternatively_spliced "Alternatively spliced") transcript variants encoding multiple isoforms have been observed.[[7]](#cite_note-entrez-7)

## Structures

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Monoamine_oxidase_A&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Structures")]

### Gene

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Monoamine_oxidase_A&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Gene")]

[Monoamine oxidase](/wiki/Monoamine_oxidase "Monoamine oxidase") A, also known as MAO-A, is an [enzyme](/wiki/Enzyme "Enzyme") that in [humans](/wiki/Human "Human") is encoded by the *MAOA* [gene](/wiki/Gene "Gene").[[5]](#cite_note-pmid1678250-5)[[6]](#cite_note-pmid2023912-6)
The promoter of *MAOA* contains conserved binding sites for [Sp1](/wiki/Sp1_transcription_factor "Sp1 transcription factor"), [GATA2](/wiki/GATA2 "GATA2"), and [TBP](/wiki/TATA_Binding_Protein "TATA Binding Protein").[[8]](#cite_note-pmid25810277-8) This gene is adjacent to a related gene (*[MAOB](/wiki/MAOB "MAOB")*) on the opposite strand of the [X chromosome](/wiki/X_chromosome "X chromosome").[[9]](#cite_note-pmid22377710-9)

In humans, there is a 30-base [repeat sequence](/wiki/Variable_number_tandem_repeat "Variable number tandem repeat") repeated several different numbers of times in the [promoter region](/wiki/Promoter_(biology) "Promoter (biology)") of MAO-A. There are 2R (two repeats), 3R, 3.5R, 4R, and 5R variants of the repeat sequence, with the 3R and 4R variants most common in all populations. The variants of the promoter have been found to appear at different frequencies in different ethnic groups in an American sample cohort.[[10]](#cite_note-pmid9799080-10)

The [epigenetic](/wiki/Epigenetic "Epigenetic") modification of *MAOA* gene expression through methylation likely plays an important role in women. A study from 2010 found epigenetic methylation of *MAOA* in men to be very low and with little variability compared to women, while having higher heritability in men than women.[[11]](#cite_note-wong-11)[[12]](#cite_note-pmid19005036-12)

### Protein

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Monoamine_oxidase_A&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Protein")]

MAO-A shares 70% [amino acid](/wiki/Amino_acid "Amino acid") sequence identity with its homologue MAO-B.[[13]](#cite_note-pmid21971000-13) Accordingly, both proteins have similar structures. Both MAO-A and MAO-B exhibit an [N-terminal](/wiki/N-terminal "N-terminal") [domain](/wiki/Protein_domain "Protein domain") that binds [flavin adenine dinucleotide](/wiki/Flavin_adenine_dinucleotide "Flavin adenine dinucleotide") (FAD), a central domain that binds the amine substrate, and a [C-terminal](/wiki/C-terminal "C-terminal") [α-helix](/wiki/Alpha-helix "Alpha-helix") that is inserted in the [outer mitochondrial membrane](/wiki/Outer_mitochondrial_membrane "Outer mitochondrial membrane").[[13]](#cite_note-pmid21971000-13)[[14]](#cite_note-Iacovino-14) MAO-A has a slightly larger substrate-binding cavity than MAO-B, which may be the cause of slight differences in catalytic activity between the two enzymes, as shown in [quantitative structure-activity relationship](/wiki/Quantitative_structure-activity_relationship "Quantitative structure-activity relationship") experiments.[[15]](#cite_note-Edmondson-15) Both enzymes are relatively large, about 60 kilodaltons in size, and are believed to function as [dimers](/wiki/Dimeric_protein "Dimeric protein") in living cells.[[14]](#cite_note-Iacovino-14)

## Function

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Monoamine_oxidase_A&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Function")]

Monoamine oxidase A catalyzes [O2](/wiki/Oxygen "Oxygen")-dependent oxidation of primary arylalkyl [amines](/wiki/Amines "Amines"), most importantly [neurotransmitters](/wiki/Neurotransmitters "Neurotransmitters") such as [serotonin](/wiki/Serotonin "Serotonin") and [dopamine](/wiki/Dopamine "Dopamine"). MAO-A primarily catabolises dopamine peripherally, rather than in the brain, which is done by [MAO-B](/wiki/Monoamine_oxidase_B "Monoamine oxidase B"). This is the initial step in the breakdown of these molecules. The products are the corresponding [aldehyde](/wiki/Aldehyde "Aldehyde"), [hydrogen peroxide](/wiki/Hydrogen_peroxide "Hydrogen peroxide"), and [ammonia](/wiki/Ammonia "Ammonia"):

:   RCH  
    2-Amine + O  
    2 + H  
    2O → R-Aldehyde + H  
    2O  
    2 + NH  
    3

This reaction is believed to occur in three steps, using FAD as an electron-transferring cofactor. First, the amine is oxidized to the corresponding [imine](/wiki/Imine "Imine"), with reduction of FAD to FADH2. Second, O2 accepts two electrons and two protons from FADH2, forming H  
2O  
2 and regenerating FAD. Third, the imine is [hydrolyzed](/wiki/Hydrolyzed "Hydrolyzed") by water, forming ammonia and the aldehyde.[[15]](#cite_note-Edmondson-15)[[16]](#cite_note-Binda-16)

Compared to MAO-B, MAO-A has a higher specificity for serotonin and [norepinephrine](/wiki/Norepinephrine "Norepinephrine"), while the two enzymes have similar affinity for dopamine and [tyramine](/wiki/Tyramine "Tyramine").[[17]](#cite_note-Kolla-17)

MAO-A is a key regulator for normal brain function. In the brain, the highest levels of [transcription](/wiki/Transcription_(biology) "Transcription (biology)") occur in the [brain stem](/wiki/Brain_stem "Brain stem"), [hypothalamus](/wiki/Hypothalamus "Hypothalamus"), [amygdala](/wiki/Amygdala "Amygdala"), [habenula](/wiki/Habenula "Habenula"), and [nucleus accumbens](/wiki/Nucleus_accumbens "Nucleus accumbens"), and the lowest in the [thalamus](/wiki/Thalamus "Thalamus"), [spinal cord](/wiki/Spinal_cord "Spinal cord"), [pituitary gland](/wiki/Pituitary_gland "Pituitary gland"), and [cerebellum](/wiki/Cerebellum "Cerebellum").[[17]](#cite_note-Kolla-17) Its expression is regulated by the [transcription factors](/wiki/Transcription_factor "Transcription factor") SP1, GATA2, and TBP via [cAMP](/wiki/Cyclic_adenosine_monophosphate "Cyclic adenosine monophosphate")-dependent regulation.[[8]](#cite_note-pmid25810277-8)[[17]](#cite_note-Kolla-17) MAO-A is also expressed in [cardiomyocytes](/wiki/Cardiomyocytes "Cardiomyocytes"), where it is induced in response to stress such as [ischemia](/wiki/Ischemia "Ischemia") and [inflammation](/wiki/Inflammation "Inflammation").[[8]](#cite_note-pmid25810277-8)

## Clinical significance

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Monoamine_oxidase_A&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Clinical significance")]

### Cancer

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Monoamine_oxidase_A&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Cancer")]

MAO-A produces an amine oxidase, which is a class of enzyme known to affect carcinogenesis. [Clorgyline](/wiki/Clorgyline "Clorgyline"), an MAO-A enzyme inhibitor, prevents [apoptosis](/wiki/Apoptosis "Apoptosis") in [melanoma](/wiki/Melanoma "Melanoma") cells, in vitro.[[18]](#cite_note-pietrangeli-18) [Cholangiocarcinoma](/wiki/Cholangiocarcinoma "Cholangiocarcinoma") suppresses MAO-A expression, and those patients with higher MAO-A expression had less adjacent organ invasion and better prognosis and survival.[[19]](#cite_note-huang-19)

### Cardiovascular disease

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Monoamine_oxidase_A&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Cardiovascular disease")]

MAOA activity is linked to [apoptosis](/wiki/Apoptosis "Apoptosis") and cardiac damage during cardiac injury following ischemic-[reperfusion](/wiki/Reperfusion_injury "Reperfusion injury").[[8]](#cite_note-pmid25810277-8)

### Behavioral and neurological disorders

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Monoamine_oxidase_A&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Behavioral and neurological disorders")]

There is some association between low activity forms of the *MAOA* gene and [autism](/wiki/Autism "Autism").[[20]](#cite_note-Cohen_2011-20) Mutations in the MAOA gene results in monoamine oxidase deficiency, or [Brunner syndrome](/wiki/Brunner_syndrome "Brunner syndrome").[[7]](#cite_note-entrez-7) Other disorders associated with MAO-A include [Alzheimer's disease](/wiki/Alzheimer%27s_disease "Alzheimer's disease"), [aggression](/wiki/Aggression "Aggression"),[*[clarification needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Please_clarify "Wikipedia:Please clarify")*] [panic disorder](/wiki/Panic_disorder "Panic disorder"), [bipolar disorder](/wiki/Bipolar_disorder "Bipolar disorder"), [major depressive disorder](/wiki/Major_depressive_disorder "Major depressive disorder"), and [attention deficit hyperactivity disorder](/wiki/Attention_deficit_hyperactivity_disorder "Attention deficit hyperactivity disorder").[[8]](#cite_note-pmid25810277-8) Effects of parenting on self-regulation in adolescents appear to be moderated by 'plasticity alleles', of which the 2R and 3R alleles of MAOA are two, with "the more plasticity alleles males (but not females) carried, the more and less self-regulation they manifested under, respectively, supportive and unsupportive parenting conditions."[[21]](#cite_note-pmid21039487-21)

#### Depression

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Monoamine_oxidase_A&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: Depression")]

MAO-A levels in the brain as measured using [positron emission tomography](/wiki/Positron_emission_tomography "Positron emission tomography") are elevated by an average of 34% in patients with [major depressive disorder](/wiki/Major_depressive_disorder "Major depressive disorder").[[22]](#cite_note-pmid17088501-22) Genetic association studies examining the relationship between high-activity *MAOA* variants and depression have produced mixed results, with some studies linking the high-activity variants to major depression in females,[[23]](#cite_note-pmid11121185-23) depressed suicide in males,[[24]](#cite_note-pmid12151768-24) major depression and sleep disturbance in males[[25]](#cite_note-pmid15486489-25) and major depressive disorder in both males and females.[[26]](#cite_note-pmid15956990-26)

Other studies failed to find a significant relationship between high-activity variants of the *MAOA* gene and major depressive disorder.[[27]](#cite_note-pmid11992558-27)[[28]](#cite_note-pmid19224413-28) In patients with major depressive disorder, those with MAOA G/T polymorphisms (rs6323) coding for the highest-activity form of the enzyme have a significantly lower magnitude of [placebo](/wiki/Placebo "Placebo") response than those with other genotypes.[[29]](#cite_note-pmid19593178-29)

#### Antisocial behavior

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Monoamine_oxidase_A&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: Antisocial behavior")]

In humans, an association between the 2R allele of the [VNTR](/wiki/Variable_number_tandem_repeat "Variable number tandem repeat") region of the gene and an increase in the likelihood of committing serious crime or violence has been found. The VNTR 2R allele of MAOA has been found to be a risk factor for violent delinquency, when present in association with stresses, i.e. family issues, low popularity or failing school.[[30]](#cite_note-30)[[31]](#cite_note-pmid18212819-31)[[32]](#cite_note-Guo_Roettger_Shih_2008-32)[[33]](#cite_note-beaver2012-33)

A connection between the *MAO-A* gene 3R version and several types of [anti-social behaviour](/wiki/Anti-social_behaviour "Anti-social behaviour") has been found: Maltreated children with genes causing high levels of MAO-A were less likely to develop antisocial behavior.[[34]](#cite_note-pmid12161658-34) Low MAO-A activity alleles which are overwhelmingly the 3R allele in combination with abuse experienced during childhood resulted in an increased risk of aggressive behaviour as an adult,[[35]](#cite_note-pmid17534436-35) and men with the low activity MAOA allele were more genetically vulnerable even to punitive discipline as a predictor of antisocial behaviour.[[36]](#cite_note-doi10.1177/2167702613518046-36) High testosterone, maternal tobacco smoking during pregnancy, poor material living standards, dropping out of school, and low IQ predicted violent behavior are associated with men with the low-activity alleles.[[37]](#cite_note-pmid22297589-37)[[38]](#cite_note-pmid17429405-38) According to a large meta-analysis in 2014, the 3R allele had a small, nonsignificant effect on aggression and antisocial behavior, in the absence of other interaction factors. Owing to methodological concerns, the authors do not view this as evidence in favor of an effect.[[39]](#cite_note-pmid24902785-39)

The *MAO-A* gene was the first candidate gene for antisocial behavior and was identified during a "molecular genetic analysis of a large, multigenerational, and notoriously violent, Dutch kindred".[[40]](#cite_note-Dorfman_2014-40) A study of Finnish prisoners revealed that a MAOA-L (low-activity) genotype, which contributes to low dopamine turnover rate, was associated with extremely violent behavior.[[41]](#cite_note-Tiihonen_2015-41) For the purpose of the study, "extremely violent behavior" was defined as at least ten committed homicides, attempted homicides or batteries.

However, a large genome-wide association study has failed to find any large or statistically significant effects of the MAOA gene on aggression.[[42]](#cite_note-42) A separate GWAS on antisocial personality disorder likewise did not report a significant effect of MAOA.[[43]](#cite_note-43) Another study, while finding effects from a candidate gene search, failed to find any evidence in a large GWAS.[[41]](#cite_note-Tiihonen_2015-41) A separate analysis of human and rat genome wide association studies, Mendelian randomization studies, and causal pathway analyses likewise failed to reveal robust evidence of MAOA in aggression.[[44]](#cite_note-44) This lack of replication is predicted from the known issues of candidate gene research, which can produce many substantial false positives.[[45]](#cite_note-45)

#### Aggression and the "Warrior gene"

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Monoamine_oxidase_A&action=edit&section=11 "Edit section: Aggression and the \"Warrior gene\"")]

Further information: [Genetics of aggression](/wiki/Genetics_of_aggression "Genetics of aggression")

Low-activity variants of the VNTR promoter region of the *MAO-A* gene have been referred to as the *warrior gene*.[[46]](#cite_note-Hogenboom-46) When faced with [social exclusion](/wiki/Social_exclusion "Social exclusion") or ostracism, individuals with the low activity *MAO-A* variants showed higher levels of aggression than individuals with the high activity *MAO-A* gene.[[47]](#cite_note-47) Low activity MAO-A could significantly predict aggressive behaviour in a high provocation situation: Individuals with the low activity variant of the *MAO-A* gene were more likely (75% as opposed to 62%, out of a sample size of 70) to retaliate, and with greater force, as compared to those with a normal *MAO-A* variant if the perceived loss was large.[[48]](#cite_note-pmid19168625-48)

The effects of MAOA genes on aggression have also been criticized for being heavily overstated.[[49]](#cite_note-Horgan-49) Indeed, the MAOA gene, even in conjunction with childhood adversity, is known to have a very small effect.[[50]](#cite_note-50) The vast majority of people with the associated alleles have not committed any violent acts.[[51]](#cite_note-51)[[52]](#cite_note-52)

#### Legal implications

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Monoamine_oxidase_A&action=edit&section=12 "Edit section: Legal implications")]

In a 2009 criminal trial in the United States, an argument based on a combination of "warrior gene" and history of [child abuse](/wiki/Child_abuse "Child abuse") was successfully used to avoid a conviction of [first-degree murder](/wiki/First-degree_murder "First-degree murder") and the death penalty; however, the convicted murderer was sentenced to 32 years in prison.[[53]](#cite_note-url_Psychology_Today-53)[[54]](#cite_note-url_NPR-54) In a second case, an individual was convicted of second-degree murder, rather than first-degree murder, based on a genetic test that revealed he had the low-activity MAOA variant.[[55]](#cite_note-55) Judges in Germany are more likely to sentence offenders to involuntary psychiatric hospitalization on hearing an accused's MAOA-L genotype.[[56]](#cite_note-56)

#### Epigenetics

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Monoamine_oxidase_A&action=edit&section=13 "Edit section: Epigenetics")]

Studies have linked methylation of the *MAOA* gene with nicotine and alcohol dependence in women.[[57]](#cite_note-pmid18454435-57) A second *MAOA* VNTR promoter, P2, influences epigenetic methylation and interacts with having experienced child abuse to influence antisocial personality disorder symptoms, only in women.[[58]](#cite_note-philibert-58) A study of 34 non-smoking men found that methylation of the gene may alter its expression in the brain.[[59]](#cite_note-59)

## Animal studies

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Monoamine_oxidase_A&action=edit&section=14 "Edit section: Animal studies")]

A dysfunctional *MAOA* gene has been correlated with increased aggression levels in mice,[[60]](#cite_note-Novel-60)[[61]](#cite_note-pmid17441000-61) and has been correlated with heightened levels of aggression in humans.[[62]](#cite_note-Abnormal-62) In mice, a dysfunctional *MAOA* gene is created through insertional mutagenesis (called 'Tg8').[[60]](#cite_note-Novel-60) Tg8 is a transgenic mouse strain that lacks functional MAO-A enzymatic activity. Mice that lacked a functional *MAOA* gene exhibited increased aggression towards intruder mice.[[60]](#cite_note-Novel-60)[[63]](#cite_note-63)

Some types of aggression exhibited by these mice were territorial aggression, predatory aggression, and isolation-induced aggression.[[61]](#cite_note-pmid17441000-61) The MAO-A deficient mice that exhibited increased isolation-induced aggression reveals that an MAO-A deficiency may also contribute to a disruption in social interactions.[[64]](#cite_note-pmid7649563-64) There is research in both humans and mice to support that a nonsense point mutation in the eighth exon of the *MAOA* gene is responsible for impulsive aggressiveness due to a complete MAO-A deficiency.[[60]](#cite_note-Novel-60)[[62]](#cite_note-Abnormal-62)

## Interactions

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Monoamine_oxidase_A&action=edit&section=15 "Edit section: Interactions")]

### Transcription factors

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Monoamine_oxidase_A&action=edit&section=16 "Edit section: Transcription factors")]

A number of [transcription factors](/wiki/Transcription_factor "Transcription factor") bind to the [promoter](/wiki/Promoter_(genetics) "Promoter (genetics)") region of MAO-A and [upregulate](/wiki/Upregulate "Upregulate") its expression. These include:[Sp1 transcription factor](/wiki/Sp1_transcription_factor "Sp1 transcription factor"), [GATA2](/wiki/GATA2 "GATA2"), [TBP](/wiki/TATA_Binding_Protein "TATA Binding Protein").[[8]](#cite_note-pmid25810277-8)

### Inducers

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Monoamine_oxidase_A&action=edit&section=17 "Edit section: Inducers")]

Synthetic compounds that up-regulate the expression of MAO-A include [Valproic acid](/wiki/Valproic_acid "Valproic acid") (Depakote)[[65]](#cite_note-mao-a-depakote-65)

### Inhibitors

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Monoamine_oxidase_A&action=edit&section=18 "Edit section: Inhibitors")]

Main articles: [Reversible inhibitor of monoamine oxidase A](/wiki/Reversible_inhibitor_of_monoamine_oxidase_A "Reversible inhibitor of monoamine oxidase A") and [Monoamine oxidase inhibitor](/wiki/Monoamine_oxidase_inhibitor "Monoamine oxidase inhibitor")

Substances that inhibit the enzymatic activity of MAO-A include:

* Synthetic compounds
  + [Befloxatone](/wiki/Befloxatone "Befloxatone") (MD370503)
  + [Brofaromine](/wiki/Brofaromine "Brofaromine") (Consonar)
  + [Cimoxatone](/wiki/Cimoxatone "Cimoxatone")
  + [Clorgyline](/wiki/Clorgyline "Clorgyline") (irreversible)
  + [Methylene Blue](/wiki/Methylene_Blue "Methylene Blue")
  + [Minaprine](/wiki/Minaprine "Minaprine") (Cantor)
  + [Moclobemide](/wiki/Moclobemide "Moclobemide") (Aurorix, Manerix)
  + [Phenelzine](/wiki/Phenelzine "Phenelzine") (Nardil)
  + [Pirlindole](/wiki/Pirlindole "Pirlindole") (Pirazidol)
  + [Toloxatone](/wiki/Toloxatone "Toloxatone") (Humoryl)
  + [Tyrima](/wiki/Tyrima "Tyrima") (CX 157)
  + [Tranylcypromine](/wiki/Tranylcypromine "Tranylcypromine") (nonselective and irreversible)
* Natural products (herbal sources)
  + [Incarviatone A](/w/index.php?title=Incarviatone_A&action=edit&redlink=1 "Incarviatone A (page does not exist)") (*[Incarvillea delavayi](/wiki/Incarvillea_delavayi "Incarvillea delavayi")*)
  + [Garlic](/wiki/Garlic "Garlic") ([Garlic](/wiki/Garlic "Garlic"))
  + [β-Carboline alkaloids](/wiki/Harmala_alkaloid "Harmala alkaloid") ([Syrian Rue](/wiki/Syrian_Rue "Syrian Rue"), [Passion Flower](/wiki/Passion_Flower "Passion Flower"), [Tobacco smoke](/wiki/Tobacco_smoke "Tobacco smoke"), [Ayahuasca](/wiki/Banisteriopsis_caapi "Banisteriopsis caapi"))
    - [Harmine](/wiki/Harmine "Harmine")
    - [Harmaline](/wiki/Harmaline "Harmaline")
  + [Isoquinoline alkaloids](/wiki/Isoquinoline_alkaloids "Isoquinoline alkaloids")
  + [Piperine](/wiki/Piperine "Piperine") ([Black pepper](/wiki/Black_pepper "Black pepper"))[[66]](#cite_note-66)
  + [Rosiridin](/wiki/Rosiridin "Rosiridin")[[67]](#cite_note-67) (*in vitro*)
